title: JTBD Analysis: Colleges, Universities, and Professional Schools (NAICS 611310)

sections.title: Phase 1 — Industry Landscape
sections.content:
### 1.1 CFJ, Output Types, Offering Level

| NAICS | CFJ | Output Types | Offering Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| 611310 | Deliver accredited higher education programs and credentials to enrolled students within a safe, legally compliant, and research-capable campus environment | Research University (R1/R2), Teaching University (M1/M2), Liberal Arts College, Community College, Professional/Technical School | L7 (Ecosystem) — output is an educational credential and learning outcome, not a physical product |

**B2C Routing check:** Not applicable. NAICS 611310 is a service operator industry. Product is a B2B component sold to campus facilities/security operations. Phase 1 routing = Operator with departments → VN node job_family.

**Factual grounding:** ~5,300 colleges and universities in the US; ~19.7M enrolled students; over one-third of campus buildings exceed 50 years old; national deferred maintenance backlog >$112B growing at 6–8% annually; NFPA codes require documented annual emergency exit inspections — single missed inspection can trigger fines exceeding $10,000 per violation. [SRC: VN_colleges_universities.md; oxmaint.com deferred maintenance report 2025; facilitiesnet.com higher education]

---

### 1.2 Segments (by circumstance, not demographics)

| Segment | Defining Circumstance | Alternative set differs? | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **S1 — Legacy building stock** | Building constructed >40 years ago; no existing wired alarm infrastructure; surface-mount install is the only viable path | Yes — wired alternatives not viable | Dominant in US public universities; >75% of campus facilities 30–40+ years old |
| **S2 — Research labs and controlled-access facilities** | High-value equipment, hazardous materials, or export-controlled research; after-hours unauthorized entry risk elevated | Yes — higher sensitivity, stricter audit requirements | R1/R2 research universities; EAR/ITAR-controlled zones |
| **S3 — Residential dormitories** | 24/7 student occupancy; elopement and unauthorized external entry risk; building occupied when security staffing is minimal | Yes — 24/7 monitoring requirement; battery life critical at high-use exits | All residential campus types |
| **S4 — High-occupancy assembly spaces** | Lecture halls, event centers, performance venues with variable, large-scale occupancy; emergency exits critical under peak load | Yes — false alarm cost high during public events | All campus types with auditoriums/arenas |
| **S5 — Open-access public buildings** | Libraries, student unions, visitor centers open to the general public; unauthorized entry difficult to detect via credential-based access alone | Yes — perimeter security less controlled; more frequent door events | All campus types |

*Target count: 5 segments. Circumstance-validated: each group faces fundamentally different alternative sets and trade-offs. Anti-pattern check passed: no demographic or budget-willingness segmentation.*

---

### 1.3 Process VN Reference

| L6 ID | Type | Name | Job Family | Türwächter Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| L6a | Core | Admissions & Enrollment | Recruit and matriculate applicants | None — no emergency exit exposure |
| L6b | Core | Academic Instruction & Curriculum | Deliver accredited coursework | Indirect — buildings hosting instruction have emergency exits |
| L6c | Core | Research & Scholarship | Generate and disseminate new knowledge | Indirect — controlled-access lab buildings (S2) |
| L6d | Core | Student Support & Campus Life | Sustain student retention and wellbeing | Indirect — dormitories (S3) and student facilities |
| L6e | Core | Assessment, Credentialing & Graduation | Evaluate performance and confer degrees | Indirect — exam halls (S4) |
| **L6-H1** | **Horizontal** | **Campus Safety, Security & Emergency Management** | **Protect all occupants and property while maintaining regulatory-compliant evacuation readiness** | **PRIMARY — Türwächter anchors at L5.h1.2 under this node** |
| L6-H2 | Horizontal | Facilities & Physical Plant Management | Maintain all campus buildings and infrastructure to code | Secondary — installation and maintenance workflows |
| L6-H3 | Horizontal | IT & Digital Infrastructure | Provide network and data infrastructure | Peripheral — IoT gateway routing via campus network |
| L6-H4 | Horizontal | Administrative, Finance & Compliance | Ensure legal, accreditation, and financial compliance | Peripheral — Clery Act compliance reporting |

*1.4 Product BOM — Skipped. NAICS 611310 is Service (Sector 61). Output is a credential and learning outcome; no physical product BOM.*
*1.5 Edges — Skipped. No Product BOM to cross-reference.*

---

sections.title: Phase 2 — Company Enters
sections.content:
### 2.1 VN Anchor

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Anchor type** | PROCESS_VN — product is used to protect campus infrastructure; it does not become part of the educational output |
| **Anchor node** | L5.h1.2 — Emergency Exit Alarm Guards |
| **Anchor level** | L5 (standalone product/system) |
| **Parent VN unit** | L6-H1 — Campus Safety, Security & Emergency Management |
| **Parent level** | L6 |
| **Anchor rationale** | The Türwächter competes directly at the L5.h1.2 slot against dormakaba SafeRoute, GEZE SecuLogic, Allegion Von Duprin alarmed exits, and basic prop alarms. It does not end up inside the educational output (credential), nor is it a platform (L6+). It is a standalone product system within the Campus Safety sub-system. |
| **Job context from parent** | L6-H1 job_family: "Protect all occupants and property across all campus operations while maintaining regulatory-compliant evacuation readiness at all times" |

---

### 2.2 Lens Formation

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Route** | Operator with departments → VN node job_family |
| **Function verb** | Protect (from L6-H1 job_family) |
| **Beneficiary** | Campus Safety, Security & Emergency Management function |
| **Lens** | "What protection does Campus Safety, Security & Emergency Management need to maintain regulatory-compliant emergency exit readiness across all building types, 24/7?" |
| **Segment filter — Level A** | L5.h1.2 serves all 5 segments (every campus building has emergency exits) |
| **Segment filter — Level B** | S1 (legacy buildings) and S3 (dormitories) have highest fit with battery-wireless retrofit; S4 (assembly spaces) has higher false-alarm sensitivity |

---

### 2.3 Core Job + Product Job

**Abstraction test:** 3+ alternatives from different categories exist (mechanical prop alarm, wired electronic exit control, access control hardware, manual patrol, do nothing) → valid Core Job.

**Product Job Statement:**
> "Help Campus Safety, Security & Emergency Management to secure emergency exit doors against unauthorized use while maintaining fire-code-compliant single-motion escape capability and reporting door status events to centralized monitoring — across distributed campus buildings with aging, heterogeneous infrastructure"

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Job type** | Product Job (L5 anchored; parent is L6, not L7) |
| **Format check** | "Help [parent unit] to [verb] [object] [context qualifier]" ✓ |
| **Technology-agnostic** | Yes — no mention of mechanical guard, piezo, EasyWave, or 868 MHz |
| **Stable over time** | Yes — regulatory mandate (NFPA 101, IBC Chapter 10, Clery Act) will not change |
| **3+ alternatives** | Yes ✓ |

---

### 2.4 Alternatives (5 per Core Job)

| # | Alternative | UNSPSC / Category | Inherent Trade-offs |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A1** | **GfS Türwächter (mechanical + IoT alarm guard)** — spring-loaded handle guard with EasyWave radio | Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guards (custom group) | EasyWave protocol lock-in (not interoperable with BACnet/KNX/campus PSIM natively); 30 m wireless range requires repeaters in large buildings; battery replacement cycle creates maintenance burden; surface-mount visible protrusion |
| **A2** | **Electronic Exit Control Systems** (dormakaba SafeRoute, GEZE SecuLogic) — mains-powered electronic exit control | 46171600 — Security and access monitoring systems | Requires mains power at door (mains wiring construction cost); fails to qualify as "fail-safe" if power-dependent alarm; high per-door installation cost; full contractor involvement; not retrofit-friendly for legacy campus buildings |
| **A3** | **Basic mechanical door prop alarms** (Detex EC-100, Seco-Larm) — contact alarm, no monitoring | 46171501 — Door and window alarms | Local alarm only (no centralized monitoring); no Clery Act–compliant documentation; no battery status visibility; no IoT; security officer must be on-site to hear alarm |
| **A4** | **Campus access control with alarmed exit hardware** (ASSA ABLOY Aperio, Allegion Von Duprin panic hardware) — credential-based control with alarmed release | 46171600 — Security and access monitoring systems | Requires full IT provisioning and credential management; high system complexity; wired infrastructure; designed for access control, not standalone emergency exit alarm monitoring; high integration cost for legacy buildings |
| **A5** | **Campus police patrol + manual inspection rounds** | N/A (labor / security services) | Zero upfront hardware cost. Requires 2–4 FTEs for full 24/7 coverage of multi-building campus; no real-time detection; compliance logs manual (error-prone); patrol frequency gaps at night; does not meet continuous monitoring expectations of fire marshals or insurance underwriters |
| — | **Do nothing / accept non-compliance** | N/A (status quo) | Zero cost. Clery Act violation risk; NFPA/state fire marshal fine exposure (>$10K/violation); insurance liability exposure; no monitoring; reputational risk at accreditation review |

*"Do nothing" included — zero P1 burden, which is why it is the most common incumbent.*

---

### 2.5 Scenarios and Output Type Activation

| Scenario | Segment | Context | Activated L6 Node | Key Constraint Active |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SC-A** | S1 — Legacy building | Retrofit deployment across 1960s–1980s concrete buildings with no existing wiring | L6-H1 + L6-H2 | C3 (wireless range), C6 (EasyWave lock-in) |
| **SC-B** | S2 — Research lab | After-hours monitoring of controlled-access lab emergency exits; EAR/ITAR compliance documentation required | L6-H1 + L6-H4 | C8 (GDPR/data logging), C6 (PSIM integration) |
| **SC-C** | S3 — Dormitory | 24/7 monitoring; high door-event frequency during student move-in/out and late nights; battery life concern | L6-H1 + L6-d | C4 (battery life at high frequency) |
| **SC-D** | S4 — Assembly space | Large lecture hall with public events; false alarm must not disrupt hundreds of occupants | L6-H1 + L6-b | False alarm sensitivity; C3 (range in large halls) |
| **SC-E** | S5 — Open public building | Library or student union; perimeter monitoring supplementing access card system | L6-H1 | C6 (PSIM integration), C8 (data compliance) |

*Sparsity check: Each scenario activates a distinct L6 pairing. No scenario activates all nodes — Core Job is appropriately scoped.*

---

sections.title: Phase 3 — Needs Derivation
sections.content:
### 3.1 Stakeholder Roles at L5.h1.2

| Role | Persona | Pyramid Levels | Scope |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Job Executor** | Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager — monitors dashboard, responds to alerts, manages devices | P1 ALL + P2 + P3 + P4 + P5 | Operates monitoring system daily |
| **Job Overseer** | VP Facilities / Chief Security Officer — accountable for Clery Act compliance, fire inspections, insurance | P1 own + P2 + P4 ALL + P5 rarely | Sees system KPIs and compliance status |
| **Product Lifecycle Support** | Installer / Facilities Maintenance Tech — installs, commissions, replaces batteries | P1 own (Preparation + Maintenance) | Stage-specific lifecycle tasks |
| **Purchase Executor** | Facilities Director / Procurement Officer — approves budget, signs contracts | P1 Acquisition | Financial metrics |
| **Purchase Influencer** | Fire Marshal / State Fire Inspector / Insurance Auditor / Campus Safety Committee | P2 own + P4 ALL | Evaluation criteria, specification standards |

---

### 3.2 P1 Product Jobs by Alternative (Executor)

| Stage | A1: Türwächter (IoT) | A2: Electronic Exit Control | A3: Basic Prop Alarm | A5: Patrol |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Acquire** | Specify EasyWave ecosystem; qualify GfS EU distributor; evaluate PSIM gateway | Full bid process; licensed electrician scope; BMS integration spec | Simple online purchase or local distributor | Hire security FTEs or contract; background checks; scheduling |
| **Prepare** | Surface-mount on existing handle; configure receiver per building; RF coverage map | Mains wiring to each door; BMS integration; programming per door | Mount contact sensor; test local alarm | Define patrol routes; train officers; log-sheet design |
| **Use** | Monitor dashboard; respond to events; manage receiver infrastructure | Manage integrated system; handle false trips | Listen for local alarm; manually patrol to confirm | Conduct patrol rounds; document findings manually |
| **Maintain** | Replace 9V battery (schedule); test EasyWave receiver connectivity | Firmware updates; wired component service | Battery replacement; physical test | Staffing continuity; officer retention; schedule coverage gaps |
| **Dispose** | Remove surface-mount unit from handle (non-destructive, <10 min) | Remove wired infrastructure (costly demolition); electrician required | Peel-off mount; discard | Terminate contracts; retrieve log books |

*Key insight: A2 dominates on Prepare burden (mains wiring = major construction). A1 (Türwächter) has unique Maintenance burden around battery + EasyWave receiver management. A5 has zero hardware burden but maximum ongoing labor burden.*

---

### 3.3 Job Map — Relevance to L5.h1.2

| Step | Name | Definition | Relevant to Türwächter | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DEFINE | Identify which campus exits require coverage; fire code specifications; campus security policy | **Yes** | Scoping errors lead to gaps in mandated coverage |
| 2 | LOCATE | Assess door hardware types, building RF environment, distance to gateway | **Yes** | RF coverage and door compatibility determine install complexity |
| 3 | PREPARE | Install and configure alarm guards; provision receivers; establish monitoring dashboard | **Yes** | Primary installation quality driver |
| 4 | CONFIRM | Verify device functionality, wireless connectivity, alarm trigger, key override | **Yes** | Silent commissioning failures are the main deployment risk |
| 5 | EXECUTE | Devices actively guard doors; alarm triggers on unauthorized activation | **Yes** | Core functional step |
| 6 | MONITOR | Dashboard aggregates door status, alarms, battery levels across campus | **Yes** | Primary value of IoT layer |
| 7 | MODIFY | Battery replacement; device add/remove; configuration updates | **Yes** | Primary operational maintenance burden |
| 8 | CONCLUDE | Compile compliance records for fire inspections, Clery Act reporting, insurance | **Yes** | Compliance documentation = key differentiator vs. basic alarms |

*All 8 steps relevant. Target: ≥2 needs per step. Total needs: 22.*

---

### 3.4 Error Statements — P1 + P2 (22 needs)

| ID | Error Statement | Pyramid | Step | Error Type | Impact | Imp | Sat | Opp | product_related | Conf |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| N01 | Minimize the likelihood of incomplete emergency exit coverage mapping that leaves fire-code-mandated campus exits unmonitored after initial deployment | P2 | DEFINE | ACCURACY | safety | 9 | 4 | **14** | false | 0.85 |
| N02 | Minimize the effort required to assess door hardware compatibility (lever vs. panic bar vs. glass-frame) and wireless signal reach across heterogeneous campus building stock before device selection | P1 Prep | LOCATE | EFFORT | cost | 8 | 5 | **11** | true | 0.80 |
| N03 | Minimize the likelihood of RF dead zones in concrete campus buildings causing emergency exit devices to transmit outside effective receiver range | P2 | LOCATE | ROBUSTNESS | reliability | 8 | 3 | **13** | true | 0.82 |
| N04 | Minimize installation labor-hours required to deploy exit monitoring devices across a multi-building campus without licensed electrical contractor involvement | P1 Prep | PREPARE | EFFORT | cost | 8 | 5 | **11** | false | 0.90 |
| N05 | Minimize variability in alarm guard installation quality across lever-handle, panic-bar, and glass-frame door configurations during a campus-wide rollout | P1 Prep | PREPARE | CONSISTENCY | reliability | 7 | 5 | **9** | false | 0.75 |
| N06 | Minimize the likelihood of deploying exit alarm devices with undetected wireless connectivity failures that are not discovered until after campus go-live | P1 Prep | CONFIRM | ACCURACY | reliability | 8 | 4 | **12** | true | 0.82 |
| N07 | Minimize the effort required to verify wireless transmission integrity between each newly installed device and the central receiver before signing off on commissioning | P1 Prep | CONFIRM | EFFORT | time | 7 | 4 | **10** | true | 0.78 |
| N08 | Minimize the likelihood of alarm guard mechanical failure in high-traffic campus exit environments (dormitories, student unions) causing an unguarded door state | P2 | EXECUTE | ROBUSTNESS | reliability | 9 | 5 | **13** | false | 0.85 |
| N09 | Minimize the likelihood of false alarm triggers caused by accidental door contact (backpacks, maintenance carts) disrupting normal building operations | P2 | EXECUTE | ROBUSTNESS | reliability | 8 | 4 | **12** | false | 0.80 |
| N10 | Minimize the time to detect and route an unauthorized emergency exit opening event to the campus security operations center | P2 | EXECUTE | DETECTION | time | 9 | 4 | **14** | false | 0.88 |
| N11 | Minimize the number of door events failing to reach the central monitoring dashboard due to RF signal attenuation in reinforced-concrete campus buildings | P1 Maint | MONITOR | DETECTION | reliability | 8 | 3 | **13** | true | 0.80 |
| N12 | Minimize variability in battery depletion rates across devices deployed in buildings with different door-use frequencies and ambient temperatures | P1 Maint | MONITOR | CONSISTENCY | reliability | 7 | 3 | **11** | true | 0.78 |
| N13 | Minimize the time between a campus emergency exit device losing wireless connectivity and the security operations center detecting the monitoring gap | P2 | MONITOR | DETECTION | time | 8 | 3 | **13** | true | 0.80 |
| N14 | Increase the ability to monitor all campus emergency exit devices from a single security dashboard without requiring building-specific logins or separate hardware systems | P2 | MONITOR | THROUGHPUT | skill | 7 | 3 | **11** | true | 0.75 |
| N15 | Minimize the time required to replace an exit alarm guard battery without triggering the alarm horn or temporarily disabling the door's fire-code escape path | P1 Maint | MODIFY | EFFORT | time | 7 | 5 | **9** | true | 0.82 |
| N16 | Minimize the time to restore full exit alarm and wireless monitoring function after a device fault or battery exhaustion event | P2 | MODIFY | RECOVERY | reliability | 8 | 4 | **12** | false | 0.80 |
| N17 | Minimize the labor required to compile emergency exit compliance documentation for annual fire safety inspections and Clery Act Annual Security Report filings | P2 | CONCLUDE | EFFORT | cost | 8 | 3 | **13** | false | 0.88 |
| N18 | Minimize the likelihood of Clery Act compliance gaps due to missing door event records from campus exit devices that experienced intermittent wireless outages | P2 | CONCLUDE | ACCURACY | safety | 9 | 3 | **15** | true | 0.85 |
| N19 | Minimize the risk of regulatory fine exposure from undocumented emergency exit non-compliance events discovered during unannounced NFPA or state fire marshal audits | P2 | CONCLUDE | ACCURACY | safety | 9 | 3 | **15** | false | 0.90 |
| N20 | Increase the ability to scale emergency exit monitoring incrementally across a campus building-by-building without system-wide infrastructure changes or full re-commissioning | P2 | DEFINE | THROUGHPUT | cost | 7 | 4 | **10** | false | 0.78 |
| N21 | Minimize the effort required to evaluate exit monitoring vendor interoperability with existing campus PSIM and Building Management Systems before procurement commitment | P1 Acq | DEFINE | EFFORT | cost | 7 | 4 | **10** | true | 0.75 |
| N22 | Minimize the likelihood of Clery Act Annual Security Report inaccuracies caused by manual compilation of door-event records that were not captured in a central digital log | P2 | CONCLUDE | ACCURACY | safety | 8 | 4 | **12** | false | 0.85 |

**Yield check:** 22 needs (within 15–30 target). product_related=true: 8/22 = 36% (target 30–40% ✓). product_related=false: 14/22 = 64% (target 60–70% ✓). Step coverage: all 8 steps represented, ≥2 needs per step ✓.

---

### 3.5 P3–P5 Bridge Questions

| Level | Role | Retrieval | Bridge | Market Need |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P3 Role** | Job Executor (Security Officer) | "By securing campus exits continuously and auto-documenting compliance, who does the security officer become?" → A proactive safety steward rather than a reactive incident responder | "What must the system do so they CAN take that proactive posture?" | Minimize the likelihood of emergency exit non-compliance being discovered during an unannounced fire marshal visit rather than through the institution's own continuous monitoring *(maps to N19)* |
| **P4 Image** | Job Overseer (VP Facilities / CSO) | "Who is watching? What must they think?" → Fire marshals, insurance underwriters, accreditation visitors must see a professionally managed, documented safety program | "What must the product do so these stakeholders SEE that impression?" | Increase the ability to generate audit-ready emergency exit compliance reports demonstrating date-stamped 100% door-event coverage for the prior 12 months, on demand within minutes *(extends N17)* |
| **P5 Emotion** | Job Executor (Security Officer) | "What do they want to avoid feeling?" → The anxiety of not knowing whether a door is propped open or has a dead alarm at 2am on the far side of campus | "What must the product do so they can avoid that anxiety?" | Minimize the likelihood of undetected after-hours emergency exit breaches remaining unresolved until the next morning's security review **[hypothesis — validate via interview]** |
| **P3 Role** | Purchase Influencer (Fire Marshal) | "By receiving digitally documented compliance evidence instead of manual logs, who does the fire marshal become?" → An inspector who can verify systemic compliance, not just spot-check | "What must the system do to enable that verification?" | Increase the ability to provide fire marshals with continuous, remotely accessible exit-status records that eliminate the need for 100% on-site physical inspection of every door *(complements N17)* |

---

### 3.6 ODI Underserved Map (opportunity ≥ 13)

| ID | Statement (abbreviated) | Opp Score | Imp | Sat | Classification |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| N18 | Clery Act compliance gaps from wireless outages | **15** | 9 | 3 | **Underserved** |
| N19 | Regulatory fine exposure from undocumented exit non-compliance | **15** | 9 | 3 | **Underserved** |
| N01 | Incomplete coverage mapping post-deployment | **14** | 9 | 4 | **Underserved** |
| N10 | Time to detect unauthorized exit opening | **14** | 9 | 4 | **Underserved** |
| N03 | RF dead zones in concrete buildings | **13** | 8 | 3 | **Underserved** |
| N08 | Mechanical failure in high-traffic dorms/unions | **13** | 9 | 5 | **Underserved** |
| N11 | Door events lost to RF attenuation | **13** | 8 | 3 | **Underserved** |
| N13 | Connectivity gap detection time | **13** | 8 | 3 | **Underserved** |
| N17 | Compliance documentation labor for inspections | **13** | 8 | 3 | **Underserved** |

**Top opportunity cluster:** Automated compliance documentation (N17, N18, N19) + detection speed (N10, N13) = the two most underserved dimensions. These map directly to the Türwächter's IoT layer differentiation (centralized dashboard + event logging). Constraint C6 (EasyWave lock-in) is the primary barrier to fulfilling N11, N13, N14 fully.

---

### QA Self-Check

| # | Check | Status |
|---|---|---|
| V1 | All statements start with "Minimize the likelihood of…" or "Increase the ability to…" with exactly ONE measurable KPI | PASS |
| V2 | product_related=true only if the need would disappear with a different technology | PASS — N03, N11-N15 are wireless/battery-specific; N06-N07 are wireless commissioning-specific |
| V3 | No unrealistic importance/satisfaction patterns; safety/regulatory needs scored 8-9 | PASS |
| V4 | Impact category matches KPI | PASS |
| V5 | ≥2 needs per relevant step; 64% market-inherent (target 60-70%), 36% technology-specific (target 30-40%) | PASS |
| V6 | Zero references to "EasyWave", "GfS", "Türwächter", "piezo", "868 MHz" in need statements | PASS |
| V7 | Anchor = L5 (standalone product/system), matches Türwächter abstraction level | PASS |
| V8 | Product Job includes parent VN unit name (Campus Safety, Security & Emergency Management) and context qualifier | PASS |

---

rows.CFJ: Deliver accredited higher education programs and credentials to enrolled students within a safe, legally compliant, and research-capable campus environment
rows.Output Types: Research University (R1/R2), Teaching University (M1/M2), Liberal Arts College, Community College, Professional/Technical School
rows.Offering Level: L7 (Ecosystem) — output is an educational credential and learning outcome, not a physical product

rows.Defining Circumstance: Building constructed >40 years ago; no existing wired alarm infrastructure; surface-mount install is the only viable path
rows.Notes: Dominant in US public universities; >75% of campus facilities 30–40+ years old

rows.Defining Circumstance: High-value equipment, hazardous materials, or export-controlled research; after-hours unauthorized entry risk elevated
rows.Alternative set differs?: Yes — higher sensitivity, stricter audit requirements

rows.Defining Circumstance: 24/7 student occupancy; elopement and unauthorized external entry risk; building occupied when security staffing is minimal
rows.Alternative set differs?: Yes — 24/7 monitoring requirement; battery life critical at high-use exits

rows.Defining Circumstance: Lecture halls, event centers, performance venues with variable, large-scale occupancy; emergency exits critical under peak load
rows.Alternative set differs?: Yes — false alarm cost high during public events

rows.Defining Circumstance: Libraries, student unions, visitor centers open to the general public; unauthorized entry difficult to detect via credential-based access alone
rows.Alternative set differs?: Yes — perimeter security less controlled; more frequent door events

rows.Name: Academic Instruction & Curriculum
rows.Türwächter Relevance: Indirect — buildings hosting instruction have emergency exits

rows.Name: **Campus Safety, Security & Emergency Management**
rows.Job Family: **Protect all occupants and property while maintaining regulatory-compliant evacuation readiness**
rows.Türwächter Relevance: **PRIMARY — Türwächter anchors at L5.h1.2 under this node**

rows.Name: Facilities & Physical Plant Management
rows.Job Family: Maintain all campus buildings and infrastructure to code

rows.Name: IT & Digital Infrastructure
rows.Türwächter Relevance: Peripheral — IoT gateway routing via campus network

rows.Name: Administrative, Finance & Compliance
rows.Job Family: Ensure legal, accreditation, and financial compliance

rows.Value: PROCESS_VN — product is used to protect campus infrastructure; it does not become part of the educational output
L6-H1 — Campus Safety, Security & Emergency Management

The Türwächter competes directly at the L5.h1.2 slot against dormakaba SafeRoute, GEZE SecuLogic, Allegion Von Duprin alarmed exits, and basic prop alarms. It does not end up inside the educational output (credential), nor is it a platform (L6+). It is a standalone product system within the Campus Safety sub-system.
L6-H1 job_family: "Protect all occupants and property across all campus operations while maintaining regulatory-compliant evacuation readiness at all times"
Operator with departments → VN node job_family
Campus Safety, Security & Emergency Management function
"What protection does Campus Safety, Security & Emergency Management need to maintain regulatory-compliant emergency exit readiness across all building types, 24/7?"
L5.h1.2 serves all 5 segments (every campus building has emergency exits)
S1 (legacy buildings) and S3 (dormitories) have highest fit with battery-wireless retrofit; S4 (assembly spaces) has higher false-alarm sensitivity
Product Job (L5 anchored; parent is L6, not L7)
Yes — no mention of mechanical guard, piezo, EasyWave, or 868 MHz
Yes — regulatory mandate (NFPA 101, IBC Chapter 10, Clery Act) will not change

rows.Alternative: **GfS Türwächter (mechanical + IoT alarm guard)** — spring-loaded handle guard with EasyWave radio
rows.UNSPSC / Category: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guards (custom group)
rows.Inherent Trade-offs: EasyWave protocol lock-in (not interoperable with BACnet/KNX/campus PSIM natively); 30 m wireless range requires repeaters in large buildings; battery replacement cycle creates maintenance burden; surface-mount visible protrusion

rows.Alternative: **Electronic Exit Control Systems** (dormakaba SafeRoute, GEZE SecuLogic) — mains-powered electronic exit control
rows.Inherent Trade-offs: Requires mains power at door (mains wiring construction cost); fails to qualify as "fail-safe" if power-dependent alarm; high per-door installation cost; full contractor involvement; not retrofit-friendly for legacy campus buildings

rows.Alternative: **Basic mechanical door prop alarms** (Detex EC-100, Seco-Larm) — contact alarm, no monitoring
rows.Inherent Trade-offs: Local alarm only (no centralized monitoring); no Clery Act–compliant documentation; no battery status visibility; no IoT; security officer must be on-site to hear alarm

rows.Alternative: **Campus access control with alarmed exit hardware** (ASSA ABLOY Aperio, Allegion Von Duprin panic hardware) — credential-based control with alarmed release
rows.Inherent Trade-offs: Requires full IT provisioning and credential management; high system complexity; wired infrastructure; designed for access control, not standalone emergency exit alarm monitoring; high integration cost for legacy buildings

Zero upfront hardware cost. Requires 2–4 FTEs for full 24/7 coverage of multi-building campus; no real-time detection; compliance logs manual (error-prone); patrol frequency gaps at night; does not meet continuous monitoring expectations of fire marshals or insurance underwriters
Zero cost. Clery Act violation risk; NFPA/state fire marshal fine exposure (>$10K/violation); insurance liability exposure; no monitoring; reputational risk at accreditation review

rows.Context: Retrofit deployment across 1960s–1980s concrete buildings with no existing wiring
After-hours monitoring of controlled-access lab emergency exits; EAR/ITAR compliance documentation required
24/7 monitoring; high door-event frequency during student move-in/out and late nights; battery life concern
Large lecture hall with public events; false alarm must not disrupt hundreds of occupants
rows.Key Constraint Active: False alarm sensitivity; C3 (range in large halls)

rows.Context: Library or student union; perimeter monitoring supplementing access card system

rows.Persona: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager — monitors dashboard, responds to alerts, manages devices
rows.Pyramid Levels: P1 ALL + P2 + P3 + P4 + P5

rows.Persona: VP Facilities / Chief Security Officer — accountable for Clery Act compliance, fire inspections, insurance
rows.Pyramid Levels: P1 own + P2 + P4 ALL + P5 rarely
rows.Scope: Sees system KPIs and compliance status

rows.Persona: Installer / Facilities Maintenance Tech — installs, commissions, replaces batteries
Facilities Director / Procurement Officer — approves budget, signs contracts
Fire Marshal / State Fire Inspector / Insurance Auditor / Campus Safety Committee

rows.A1: Türwächter (IoT): Specify EasyWave ecosystem; qualify GfS EU distributor; evaluate PSIM gateway
rows.A2: Electronic Exit Control: Full bid process; licensed electrician scope; BMS integration spec
rows.A3: Basic Prop Alarm: Simple online purchase or local distributor
rows.A5: Patrol: Hire security FTEs or contract; background checks; scheduling

rows.A1: Türwächter (IoT): Surface-mount on existing handle; configure receiver per building; RF coverage map
rows.A2: Electronic Exit Control: Mains wiring to each door; BMS integration; programming per door
rows.A3: Basic Prop Alarm: Mount contact sensor; test local alarm
rows.A5: Patrol: Define patrol routes; train officers; log-sheet design

rows.A1: Türwächter (IoT): Monitor dashboard; respond to events; manage receiver infrastructure
rows.A2: Electronic Exit Control: Manage integrated system; handle false trips
rows.A3: Basic Prop Alarm: Listen for local alarm; manually patrol to confirm
rows.A5: Patrol: Conduct patrol rounds; document findings manually

rows.A1: Türwächter (IoT): Replace 9V battery (schedule); test EasyWave receiver connectivity
rows.A5: Patrol: Staffing continuity; officer retention; schedule coverage gaps

rows.A1: Türwächter (IoT): Remove surface-mount unit from handle (non-destructive, <10 min)
rows.A2: Electronic Exit Control: Remove wired infrastructure (costly demolition); electrician required

rows.Name: DEFINE
rows.Definition: Identify which campus exits require coverage; fire code specifications; campus security policy
rows.Rationale: Scoping errors lead to gaps in mandated coverage

rows.Name: LOCATE
rows.Definition: Assess door hardware types, building RF environment, distance to gateway
rows.Rationale: RF coverage and door compatibility determine install complexity

rows.Name: PREPARE
rows.Definition: Install and configure alarm guards; provision receivers; establish monitoring dashboard

rows.Name: CONFIRM
rows.Definition: Verify device functionality, wireless connectivity, alarm trigger, key override
rows.Rationale: Silent commissioning failures are the main deployment risk

rows.Name: EXECUTE
rows.Definition: Devices actively guard doors; alarm triggers on unauthorized activation

rows.Name: MONITOR
rows.Definition: Dashboard aggregates door status, alarms, battery levels across campus

rows.Name: MODIFY
rows.Definition: Battery replacement; device add/remove; configuration updates

rows.Name: CONCLUDE
rows.Definition: Compile compliance records for fire inspections, Clery Act reporting, insurance
rows.Rationale: Compliance documentation = key differentiator vs. basic alarms

rows.Error Statement: Minimize the likelihood of incomplete emergency exit coverage mapping that leaves fire-code-mandated campus exits unmonitored after initial deployment
Minimize the effort required to assess door hardware compatibility (lever vs. panic bar vs. glass-frame) and wireless signal reach across heterogeneous campus building stock before device selection
Minimize the likelihood of RF dead zones in concrete campus buildings causing emergency exit devices to transmit outside effective receiver range
Minimize installation labor-hours required to deploy exit monitoring devices across a multi-building campus without licensed electrical contractor involvement
Minimize variability in alarm guard installation quality across lever-handle, panic-bar, and glass-frame door configurations during a campus-wide rollout
Minimize the likelihood of deploying exit alarm devices with undetected wireless connectivity failures that are not discovered until after campus go-live
Minimize the effort required to verify wireless transmission integrity between each newly installed device and the central receiver before signing off on commissioning
Minimize the likelihood of alarm guard mechanical failure in high-traffic campus exit environments (dormitories, student unions) causing an unguarded door state
Minimize the likelihood of false alarm triggers caused by accidental door contact (backpacks, maintenance carts) disrupting normal building operations
Minimize the time to detect and route an unauthorized emergency exit opening event to the campus security operations center
Minimize the number of door events failing to reach the central monitoring dashboard due to RF signal attenuation in reinforced-concrete campus buildings
Minimize variability in battery depletion rates across devices deployed in buildings with different door-use frequencies and ambient temperatures
Minimize the time between a campus emergency exit device losing wireless connectivity and the security operations center detecting the monitoring gap
Increase the ability to monitor all campus emergency exit devices from a single security dashboard without requiring building-specific logins or separate hardware systems
Minimize the time required to replace an exit alarm guard battery without triggering the alarm horn or temporarily disabling the door's fire-code escape path
Minimize the time to restore full exit alarm and wireless monitoring function after a device fault or battery exhaustion event
Minimize the labor required to compile emergency exit compliance documentation for annual fire safety inspections and Clery Act Annual Security Report filings
Minimize the likelihood of Clery Act compliance gaps due to missing door event records from campus exit devices that experienced intermittent wireless outages
Minimize the risk of regulatory fine exposure from undocumented emergency exit non-compliance events discovered during unannounced NFPA or state fire marshal audits
Increase the ability to scale emergency exit monitoring incrementally across a campus building-by-building without system-wide infrastructure changes or full re-commissioning
Minimize the effort required to evaluate exit monitoring vendor interoperability with existing campus PSIM and Building Management Systems before procurement commitment
Minimize the likelihood of Clery Act Annual Security Report inaccuracies caused by manual compilation of door-event records that were not captured in a central digital log

rows.Retrieval: "By securing campus exits continuously and auto-documenting compliance, who does the security officer become?" → A proactive safety steward rather than a reactive incident responder
rows.Bridge: "What must the system do so they CAN take that proactive posture?"
rows.Market Need: Minimize the likelihood of emergency exit non-compliance being discovered during an unannounced fire marshal visit rather than through the institution's own continuous monitoring *(maps to N19)*

rows.Retrieval: "Who is watching? What must they think?" → Fire marshals, insurance underwriters, accreditation visitors must see a professionally managed, documented safety program
rows.Bridge: "What must the product do so these stakeholders SEE that impression?"
rows.Market Need: Increase the ability to generate audit-ready emergency exit compliance reports demonstrating date-stamped 100% door-event coverage for the prior 12 months, on demand within minutes *(extends N17)*

rows.Retrieval: "What do they want to avoid feeling?" → The anxiety of not knowing whether a door is propped open or has a dead alarm at 2am on the far side of campus
rows.Bridge: "What must the product do so they can avoid that anxiety?"
rows.Market Need: Minimize the likelihood of undetected after-hours emergency exit breaches remaining unresolved until the next morning's security review **[hypothesis — validate via interview]**

rows.Retrieval: "By receiving digitally documented compliance evidence instead of manual logs, who does the fire marshal become?" → An inspector who can verify systemic compliance, not just spot-check
rows.Bridge: "What must the system do to enable that verification?"
rows.Market Need: Increase the ability to provide fire marshals with continuous, remotely accessible exit-status records that eliminate the need for 100% on-site physical inspection of every door *(complements N17)*

rows.Statement (abbreviated): Clery Act compliance gaps from wireless outages
Regulatory fine exposure from undocumented exit non-compliance
Time to detect unauthorized exit opening
RF dead zones in concrete buildings
Door events lost to RF attenuation

rows.Check: All statements start with "Minimize the likelihood of…" or "Increase the ability to…" with exactly ONE measurable KPI
product_related=true only if the need would disappear with a different technology
rows.Status: PASS — N03, N11-N15 are wireless/battery-specific; N06-N07 are wireless commissioning-specific

rows.Check: No unrealistic importance/satisfaction patterns; safety/regulatory needs scored 8-9
≥2 needs per relevant step; 64% market-inherent (target 60-70%), 36% technology-specific (target 30-40%)
Zero references to "EasyWave", "GfS", "Türwächter", "piezo", "868 MHz" in need statements
Anchor = L5 (standalone product/system), matches Türwächter abstraction level
Product Job includes parent VN unit name (Campus Safety, Security & Emergency Management) and context qualifier

phase1.cfj: Deliver accredited higher education programs and credentials to enrolled students within a safe, legally compliant, and research-capable campus environment

segments.name: Legacy Building Stock

segments.name: Residential Dormitories

vn_anchor.parent_vn_unit: L6-H1 — Campus Safety, Security & Emergency Management

phase2.lens: What protection does Campus Safety, Security & Emergency Management need to maintain regulatory-compliant emergency exit readiness across all campus building types, 24/7?
phase2.product_job_statement: Help Campus Safety, Security & Emergency Management to secure emergency exit doors against unauthorized use while maintaining fire-code-compliant single-motion escape capability and reporting door status events to centralized monitoring — across distributed campus buildings with aging, heterogeneous infrastructure

alternatives.name: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guards (IoT variant)
alternatives.category: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guards (custom group)
trade_offs: EasyWave protocol lock-in — not interoperable with campus PSIM natively
30 m wireless range requires repeaters in large buildings
Surface-mount visible protrusion on door handle

alternatives.name: Electronic Exit Control Systems (mains-powered)
trade_offs: Mains power wiring required at each door — major construction in legacy buildings
High per-door installation cost; licensed contractor required
Single point of failure if power-dependent alarm

alternatives.name: Basic Mechanical Door Prop Alarms
trade_offs: Local alarm only — no centralized monitoring

alternatives.name: Campus Access Control with Alarmed Exit Hardware
trade_offs: Full IT provisioning and credential management required
Wired infrastructure; high integration cost for legacy buildings
Designed for access control, not standalone exit alarm monitoring

alternatives.name: Campus Police Patrol + Manual Inspection Rounds

steps.statement: Identify which campus exit doors require alarm guard coverage per NFPA 101, IBC Chapter 10, and campus security policy
Assess door hardware types, building RF environment, and distance from doors to wireless gateway
Install and configure alarm guards; provision wireless receivers; establish campus monitoring dashboard
Verify device functionality, wireless transmission, alarm trigger, and key override before go-live
Alarm guards continuously secure exit doors; alarm triggers on unauthorized activation
Centralized dashboard aggregates door status, alarm events, and battery levels across all campus buildings
Replace batteries, respond to alarm events, add/remove devices as campus footprint changes
Compile compliance documentation for annual fire safety inspections and Clery Act Annual Security Report
